summary: Before the COVID-19 pandemic hit, I worked as a voice and piano teacher at Annie Wright Schools in Tacoma. I had about 45 students between Annie Wright and another music academy, so it was a busy and fulfilling teaching role. My work there was a significant part of my music career before everything shut down in early 2020.

Tacoma is where I lived and worked before the pandemic, teaching at Annie Wright Schools and Cappella Music Academy. It’s also where I performed regularly with Symphony Tacoma Voices and Tacoma Opera. My music life was deeply rooted in Tacoma, especially leading up to early 2020 when the pandemic caused everything to shut down.

I worked as a voice and piano teacher at Cappella Music Academy alongside Annie Wright Schools before the pandemic. I had a good number of students there, contributing to my livelihood through teaching. Cappella was an important part of my teaching career in Tacoma prior to the shutdown in early 2020.

I was a regular performer with Symphony Tacoma Voices before the pandemic, and I had just secured my first solo with them in a commissioned work. We were rehearsing right up until the shutdown in March 2020. The choir experience was very close to home when the Skagit Valley Chorale COVID-19 outbreak happened, making us realize how lucky we were to avoid infection.

Right before the pandemic shutdown, I had just landed my first named role with Tacoma Opera, playing Fleta in Iolanthe. It was a big milestone for me as a soprano, and I was really excited about performing, even keeping my pink hair on stage. Unfortunately, the production was canceled due to COVID-19, and I lost that contract, but Tacoma Opera later set up an Artists Relief Fund to help those affected.

Iolanthe was the opera I was cast in with Tacoma Opera just as the pandemic hit. I was set to play Fleta, a named role, which was a big step up for me. We were about to start rehearsals in March 2020, but everything shut down around St. Patrick’s Day, so the production never happened.

Iolanthe, the opera I was cast in, is a work by Gilbert and Sullivan. Their operettas are well-known, and I was thrilled to be part of a production of theirs with Tacoma Opera. It was a meaningful opportunity that was unfortunately cut short by the pandemic.

St. Patrick’s Day 2020 was a pivotal moment when everything in Washington state, including music rehearsals and performances, shut down due to COVID-19. We were still rehearsing with Symphony Tacoma Voices that week, and the news of the Skagit Valley Chorale outbreak came out around then. It was a sobering and surreal time for all of us in the music community.

The COVID-19 pandemic completely changed my music career starting in early 2020. I had just achieved major milestones like a named role in Tacoma Opera and a solo with Symphony Tacoma Voices, but everything shut down around March. The pandemic also made singing a risky activity due to aerosol spread, which deeply affected choirs and vocalists like me. Financially, I lost some contracts but was able to continue teaching online and received some relief funds.

The Skagit Valley Chorale COVID-19 outbreak was a tragic super-spreading event in Washington state where one infected person unknowingly transmitted the virus to most of the choir. About two-thirds of the choir got infected and two members died. This event happened just as we were still rehearsing with Symphony Tacoma Voices, and it was a heavy, emotional realization of how dangerous singing could be during the pandemic.

entity: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
summary: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention studied the Skagit Valley Chorale outbreak and provided important data on how COVID-19 spreads in choir settings. Their research helped us understand the risks of singing in groups during the pandemic. This information was crucial for making decisions about rehearsals and performances in Washington state.
cues: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

summary: Seattle was part of the region affected early by COVID-19, and I accessed a Seattle area musicians relief fund after losing performing opportunities. The city’s music community was impacted alongside Tacoma and the greater Washington area. Relief efforts in Seattle helped musicians like me manage the financial challenges brought on by the pandemic.
